content_markdown: A wire of length $L$ is used in an electric heater. When the potential difference across the wire is $200\text{ V}$, the power dissipated in the wire is $1000\text{ W}$. The same potential difference is applied across a second similar wire of length $2L$. What is the power dissipated in the second wire?
vibe_explanation: Resistance $R$ is proportional to length $L$ ($R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$). Doubling the length doubles the resistance. Since power $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ and the potential difference $V$ remains constant, doubling the resistance results in the power being halved ($1000\text{ W} / 2 = 500\text{ W}$).
